title: Type affinity and flexible typing
description: SQLite columns have affinity, not strict types, so a column can hold values of any type unless constrained.
body: Use when data comes back the wrong type or a constraint seems ignored. SQLite uses dynamic typing: a column's declared type gives it an affinity (TEXT, NUMERIC, INTEGER, REAL, BLOB) that nudges conversion, but by default any column can store any type, so a string can land in an INTEGER column. Do not assume the declared type enforces storage type. Actions: use a STRICT table (modern SQLite) to enforce declared types, add CHECK constraints, and validate/convert in application code; know that INTEGER PRIMARY KEY aliases the rowid. Validation signals include a STRICT-table test rejecting a wrong-typed insert. Risk boundary: relying on affinity for data integrity lets malformed values persist silently — a correctness finding.
